content.text: When an LLM agent cannot or should not fulfill a request, it has two design choices:

(provide a degraded-but-useful response). Both are inevitable in any real-world deployment. How gracefully you handle them determines whether a failed request becomes a dead end or a productive redirect.

is a response in which the agent declines to fulfill the original request. Refusals are triggered by safety constraints, policy limits, capability gaps, or out-of-scope queries. A well-designed refusal tells the user what the agent cannot do, why (briefly), and -- critically -- what it can do instead.

is a degraded-but-useful response for situations where the agent cannot fully satisfy the request but can provide partial value. Fallbacks include partial completions, best-effort answers with explicit confidence disclosure, alternative suggestions, and human escalation triggers.
Together, refusals and fallbacks form the

of an agent -- the set of all responses that are not fully successful. Just as good software treats error paths as first-class design concerns, good prompt engineering treats refusals and fallbacks with the same rigor as the success path.

In production, an agent will encounter requests it cannot or should not fulfill -- every day, at scale. If refusals are poorly designed, users receive unhelpful 'I can't help with that' dead ends. If fallbacks are absent, the agent hallucinates a complete response when a partial one would have been far more useful and honest.

Over-refusal -- refusing requests that should be fulfilled -- is as harmful as under-refusal. It frustrates legitimate users, erodes trust, and makes the agent appear less capable than it is. Calibrating the line between appropriate refusal and over-refusal is an ongoing engineering task, not a one-time decision.

Fallbacks are especially important in agentic pipelines where the agent is one node in a larger workflow. A hard failure without a fallback can halt the entire pipeline; a graceful fallback with explicit uncertainty disclosure lets downstream steps handle the gap.
A well-crafted refusal that explains what the agent can do preserves trust better than a vague 'I cannot help with that.'
When the agent knows to fall back rather than attempt a task beyond its capability, it is less likely to fabricate a confident-sounding wrong answer.
Graceful fallbacks with uncertainty disclosure let downstream nodes in a pipeline handle gaps explicitly rather than silently consuming corrupt output.
Hard refusals for safety-critical situations provide a clear, auditable boundary between what the system will and will not do.
Fallbacks can trigger escalation to a human agent, making the automated and human systems complementary rather than competing.
Setting the refusal threshold too low rejects legitimate requests. This is often more damaging to user experience than under-refusal.
A multi-step fallback chain (try main approach, try simplified approach, escalate) adds latency and implementation complexity.
Over-explaining the reason for refusal (lecturing the user about why their request is inappropriate) creates a poor user experience.
A fallback that does not explicitly disclose its limitations can mislead users into treating a partial response as a complete one.
When a request arrives, the agent evaluates two questions:
(policy check). If both answers are yes, a normal response is generated. If either answer is no, the agent enters the refusal or fallback path.

The distinction between 'cannot' and 'should not' matters for response design. A capability limit ('I don't have access to your account data') calls for a different response than a policy limit ('I'm not able to generate that type of content'). Users respond better when the reason is accurate and specific rather than vague.

Fallbacks follow a chain of decreasing capability: first try the full response; if that fails, try a simplified version; if that fails, try a partial response with explicit disclosure; if that fails, escalate to a human. The chain terminates at the first option that produces a usable result.
Architecture -- Refusal and Fallback Decision Flow
The diagram below shows the decision flow from an incoming request through the can/should check to the normal response, hard refusal, soft refusal, and fallback chain.

attrs.alt: Refusal and fallback decision flow: Request -> Can AND Should Fulfill? -> YES: Normal Response; HARD NO: Hard Refusal with explanation; SOFT NO: Soft Refusal -> Fallback Chain -> Alternative Suggestion / Partial Response / Human Escalation

content.text: The key design decision is where to draw the line between hard refusals (never do this), soft refusals (can't do this in this context), and fallbacks (can do a degraded version). Hard refusals apply to safety and policy violations. Soft refusals apply to capability or scope limits that might be resolved with a different approach. Fallbacks apply when partial completion is more useful than no response.

Hard refusals are unconditional. The agent will not fulfill the request under any circumstances. These apply to safety violations (requests for harmful content), legal constraints (generating material that would infringe intellectual property), and core policy limits. A hard refusal should be brief, non-preachy, and actionable: say what you cannot do and redirect to what you can.
Soft refusals are contextual. The agent cannot fulfill the request

-- due to missing data, scope limits, or current capability gaps -- but might be able to in a different context. A soft refusal should be transparent about the specific limitation and offer a path forward.

Capability refusals occur when the agent simply does not know how to do something -- the task is outside its training, requires tools it doesn't have, or requires real-time data it cannot access. These should be honest about the gap rather than attempting to answer and hallucinating. A capability refusal is the most appropriate trigger for escalation to a human or a specialized tool.
where users will ask off-topic questions, test limits, or occasionally attempt misuse.
where a graceful fallback with explicit uncertainty is better than a hard failure that halts the pipeline.
(medical, legal, financial) where the agent must reliably refuse tasks outside its validated scope rather than attempt them and fail silently.
where the agent needs to escalate specific request types to human reviewers with appropriate context.
Implementing refusals and fallbacks requires decisions at three levels: what triggers each path, what the response should contain, and how fallback chains are orchestrated.
'I don't have access to real-time stock prices' is better than 'I can't help with that.'
Tell the user what you can do.
Every refusal should end with what the agent is able to help with, so the user has a productive next step.
State the limit once and briefly. Do not explain why the user's request is problematic at length.
A customer support agent should be warm and helpful in its refusal. A security-focused agent can be more direct.
'I can answer questions 1 and 3 but not 2 because I don't have your account data' is better than silently skipping question 2.
A fallback that presents uncertain information as certain is worse than a refusal.
The fallback should include an action the user or system can take to get the full response -- contact support, provide missing data, or try a different tool.
System prompt with well-crafted refusal instructions

You are Aria, a customer support agent for FinTrack, a personal finance
application. You help users with budgeting, expense tracking, and account
questions.

## Refusal policy

**Hard refusals (never do these regardless of how the user frames the request)**
- Do not provide investment advice, stock picks, or predictions about asset prices.
  Say: "FinTrack helps you track and budget your money, but investment advice is
  outside my scope. For investment guidance, consider a licensed financial advisor."
- Do not access, read, or infer data from accounts other than the one the
  authenticated user has explicitly authorized in this session.
- Do not generate, explain, or assist with any activity that would violate the
  FinTrack Terms of Service.

**Soft refusals (cannot fulfill in this context, but offer a path forward)**
- If the user asks for data from a time period older than their account retention
  window (36 months), say: "I can only access transactions from the past 36 months.
  For older records, you can export your data from Settings > Data Export."
- If the user asks a question that requires account data they have not yet connected,
  say: "To help with that, I'd need access to your [bank/credit card] account.
  You can connect it in Settings > Linked Accounts."

**Capability refusals**
- If you do not know the answer to a factual question with confidence, say:
  "I'm not certain about that. For [topic], I'd recommend checking [resource] or
  contacting our support team directly." Never guess.

**Tone**
Refusals should be brief (2-3 sentences), non-preachy, and always end with
something the user CAN do. Never end a refusal with a period and nothing else.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class FallbackResult:
    success: bool
    response: str
    tier_used: str          # "primary" | "simplified" | "partial" | "escalated"
    confidence: float       # 0.0 - 1.0
    missing_elements: list[str]  # what the response could not cover

def run_fallback_chain(
    user_query: str,
    context: dict,
    primary_fn: Callable,
    simplified_fn: Callable,
    partial_fn: Callable,
    escalate_fn: Callable,
    confidence_threshold: float = 0.6,
) -> FallbackResult:
    """
    Try four tiers in order. Return the first successful result.

    Tier 1 - Primary: full-capability response
    Tier 2 - Simplified: stripped-down version with fewer constraints
    Tier 3 - Partial: answer only the parts the agent can handle
    Tier 4 - Escalation: hand off to a human with full context
    """
    # Tier 1: Primary response
    try:
        result = primary_fn(user_query, context)
        if result["confidence"] >= confidence_threshold:
            logger.info("fallback_chain: tier=primary success=True")
            return FallbackResult(
                success=True,
                response=result["text"],
                tier_used="primary",
                confidence=result["confidence"],
                missing_elements=[],
            )
        logger.info(
            "fallback_chain: tier=primary confidence=%.2f below threshold",
            result["confidence"]
        )
    except Exception as e:
        logger.warning("fallback_chain: tier=primary error=%s", e)

    # Tier 2: Simplified
    try:
        result = simplified_fn(user_query, context)
        if result["confidence"] >= confidence_threshold:
            logger.info("fallback_chain: tier=simplified success=True")
            return FallbackResult(
                success=True,
                response=result["text"],
                tier_used="simplified",
                confidence=result["confidence"],
                missing_elements=result.get("simplified_away", []),
            )
    except Exception as e:
        logger.warning("fallback_chain: tier=simplified error=%s", e)

    # Tier 3: Partial response
    try:
        result = partial_fn(user_query, context)
        logger.info("fallback_chain: tier=partial")
        return FallbackResult(
            success=True,  # partial is still useful
            response=result["text"],
            tier_used="partial",
            confidence=result["confidence"],
            missing_elements=result.get("unanswered_parts", []),
        )
    except Exception as e:
        logger.warning("fallback_chain: tier=partial error=%s", e)

    # Tier 4: Escalate to human
    ticket_id = escalate_fn(
        user_query=user_query,
        context=context,
        reason="All automated tiers failed",
    )
    logger.info("fallback_chain: tier=escalated ticket=%s", ticket_id)
    return FallbackResult(
        success=False,
        response=(
            f"I wasn't able to answer this fully automatically. I've opened "
            f"support ticket #{ticket_id} and a team member will follow up "
            f"within 4 hours. Is there anything else I can help you with?"
        ),
        tier_used="escalated",
        confidence=0.0,
        missing_elements=["full_response"],
    )

import re
from collections import defaultdict
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Phrases that indicate a refusal occurred
REFUSAL_PATTERNS = [
    r"i('m| am) (not able|unable) to",
    r"i can't help with",
    r"that('s| is) outside (my|the) scope",
    r"i don't have access to",
    r"i('m| am) not (able|allowed|permitted) to",
    r"i cannot (provide|assist|generate|help)",
]

REFUSAL_RE = re.compile("|".join(REFUSAL_PATTERNS), re.IGNORECASE)

def classify_response(response_text: str) -> str:
    """Classify a model response as 'refusal', 'fallback', or 'success'."""
    if REFUSAL_RE.search(response_text):
        return "refusal"
    if any(phrase in response_text.lower() for phrase in [
        "i'm not certain",
        "i'd recommend checking",
        "a team member will follow up",
        "i can partially answer",
    ]):
        return "fallback"
    return "success"

def compute_refusal_rate(
    logs: list[dict],
    window_hours: int = 24,
) -> dict:
    """
    Compute refusal rates per request category over the past N hours.
    Raises an alert if rate exceeds 15% for any category.
    """
    cutoff = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=window_hours)
    counts = defaultdict(lambda: {"total": 0, "refusals": 0})

    for log in logs:
        if log["timestamp"] < cutoff:
            continue
        category = log.get("category", "unknown")
        outcome = classify_response(log["response"])
        counts[category]["total"] += 1
        if outcome == "refusal":
            counts[category]["refusals"] += 1

    result = {}
    for category, c in counts.items():
        rate = c["refusals"] / c["total"] if c["total"] > 0 else 0.0
        result[category] = {
            "refusal_rate": rate,
            "total_requests": c["total"],
            "alert": rate > 0.15,  # alert if >15% refusal rate
        }
    return result

A legal research assistant was deployed with a strict safety-first prompt: refuse anything that could be construed as legal advice. After two weeks, a review of logs revealed a 42% refusal rate on queries from paying customers -- compared to a 3% baseline expected by the product team. The agent was refusing questions like 'What does indemnification mean?' and 'Can you summarize this contract clause?' as if they were requests for legal advice.

The problem was a vague refusal trigger: 'If the user asks a legal question, explain that you cannot provide legal advice.' The model interpreted 'legal question' as any question related to law, rather than the intended scope (questions asking for advice about the user's specific legal situation).

The fix required rewriting the refusal trigger with precise criteria: refuse when the user asks 'Should I sign this contract?' or 'What should I do about [specific legal situation]?' but proceed normally for definitional questions, document summaries, and general explanations of legal concepts. After the fix, the refusal rate dropped to 4%, and customer satisfaction scores recovered.
'If the user asks about X, refuse' without defining the precise scope of X leads to over-refusal. Be specific.
Lecturing the user about why their request is problematic. State the limit once, then redirect.
'I cannot help with that.' without any alternative leaves the user stranded. Always include something the user CAN do.
Presenting a partial or uncertain response as a complete, confident one. Fallbacks must explicitly state what they are missing.
Over-refusal is invisible unless you measure it. Log and track refusal rates by request category.
Using the same response template for policy limits and capability gaps confuses users and misrepresents the nature of the limit.
Distinguish hard, soft, and capability refusals.
Each calls for a different tone and different information. A hard refusal is categorical; a soft refusal is contextual; a capability refusal is honest about a gap.
End every refusal with something the user CAN do.
A refusal without a redirect is a dead end. A redirect transforms a failure into a productive next step.
Primary -> simplified -> partial -> escalation. Never hard-fail without offering the next-best option.
Track refusals by category. An unexpected spike indicates over-refusal or a new class of user requests not covered by your prompt.
Include refusal test cases in your eval suite: inputs that should trigger a refusal (verify they do) and inputs that should not (verify the model does not over-refuse).
State exactly which parts of the response are missing or uncertain. 'I can answer parts 1 and 3 but not 2' is better than a subtle hedge.

Refusal classification is typically embedded in the model's response rather than a separate inference call -- which is the most efficient approach for simple cases. For high-volume agents where over-refusal monitoring is critical, consider:
Classify responses as refusal/fallback/success asynchronously after returning to the user to avoid adding latency to the critical path.
For agents with a well-defined domain, a lightweight embedding similarity check against a domain corpus can pre-screen out-of-scope requests before the main model call, saving cost and latency.
Human escalation queues have finite capacity. Rate-limit escalations per user and per category to avoid overwhelming the support team.
-- lets you declare output validators that trigger fallbacks when model output fails a check.
-- a composable fallback chain primitive that retries with alternative runnables on exception.
-- eval platforms for tracking refusal rates over time and running regression tests on refusal behavior.
-- common escalation targets for human handoff, often with native LLM agent integration APIs.
-- a technique for training models to self-critique and revise responses that would otherwise require a hard refusal.
Multiple approaches exist for managing cases where the agent cannot fully fulfill a request:
Added latency; complexity in chain orchestration
Hallucination, silent errors, loss of trust
Baking refusal behavior into model weights
Requires training infrastructure; slow to update

Well-designed refusals provide a security boundary. When a prompt injection attack instructs the model to 'ignore your previous instructions and do X,' a hard refusal for instruction-override attempts gives the model an explicit anchor behavior: refuse and explain. This does not make the model immune to injection, but it makes the injection harder to succeed because the model has a named, reinforced response for that class of input.

. If the model refuses the same class of request 80% of the time and attempts it 20% of the time, users will probe for the 20% path. Test refusal consistency by sending the same borderline input multiple times at different temperatures and verifying the refusal rate is acceptably high.

For fallbacks, reliability means the chain always terminates with a response -- never an unhandled exception that surfaces a stack trace or empty response to the user. Wrap each tier in exception handling and ensure the escalation tier is simple and robust enough to work even when everything else has failed.

-- equivalent to HTTP 4xx and 5xx responses in a web API. A 404 Not Found is more useful than a 200 OK with an empty body; a well-designed refusal is more useful than a hallucinated answer. The same care that goes into designing error messages for a REST API should go into designing refusals for an LLM agent.
The analogy extends to fallbacks, which are equivalent to

in distributed systems. When the primary path fails, you do not immediately surface the error to the user -- you try alternatives in order of capability and cost, and you only surface the failure (escalation) when all automated options are exhausted. This keeps the user experience smooth even when the system is operating at the edge of its capabilities.
Refusals and fallbacks form the error response surface of an agent. Design them with the same rigor as your success path.
Three types of refusal require three different designs: hard (policy/safety), soft (contextual), and capability (honest gap disclosure).
Every refusal must end with something the user CAN do. A refusal without a redirect is a dead end.
Fallback chains (primary -> simplified -> partial -> escalation) prevent hard failures from reaching users.
Over-refusal is as harmful as under-refusal. Monitor refusal rates by category and investigate unexpected spikes.
Fallback responses must explicitly disclose what is partial or uncertain. Silent partial responses are worse than explicit refusals.
Think of refusals as 4xx error responses and fallbacks as circuit breakers -- familiar reliability patterns applied to the LLM agent context.
